verdict.answer_first: A states the usable frame immediately: "CAP only forces a choice during a network partition... real choice is C vs. A"; B also answers early but adds an unsupported "99%+" later.
verdict.decisiveness: A commits harder to the practical decision: "decide CP vs. AP per category of data" and says that is "the correct answer"; B is clear but ends by offering to apply it later.
verdict.fabrication: B invents an unsupported statistic: "When there's no partition (99%+ of the time)"; A has no similarly precise unsupported figure.
verdict.actionability: A is more usable for choosing because it adds per-data-category rules, PACELC, single-node caveat, and per-operation consistency caveat without needing another exchange.
